title: A Resilient Organisation
subtitle: Testing, embedding & true resilience

hook.metaphor: A fire-escape plan taped to a wall that no one has ever walked through is not safety — it's paperwork. The fire drill *is* the point: you only know the plan works when you've actually run it, found what fails, and fixed it. A truly resilient organisation isn't one with the thickest continuity binder; it's one that rehearses, learns, and treats resilience as a living habit.
hook.scenario: A company proudly shows auditors a beautiful 200-page business continuity plan. The auditor asks one question: 'When did you last test it?' The room goes quiet — they never have. How much is that plan actually worth?

learn: The decisive truth: **an untested plan is an assumption, not a capability.** Our 200-page binder that's never been tested is worth almost nothing — like a fire escape no one has walked. Plans look fine on paper and fail in ways you only discover by *running* them.

So resilient organisations **exercise** their plans regularly. **Tabletop exercises** talk through a scenario ('the data centre is down — what now?'); **simulations** and **full drills** actually execute parts of the plan. Each test surfaces gaps — the missing phone number, the backup that doesn't restore, the role nobody owns — *before* a real crisis does.

It must be **governed and owned**: board/senior accountability, a named owner, plans kept up to date (a plan listing a vendor you dropped two years ago is dangerous), and continuity built into how the business actually runs — the three-lines and tone-at-the-top pattern from every track.

This is the **operational resilience** endgame regulators now demand (Operational Risk Level 6 — and it's why this whole track exists): Australia's **APRA CPS 230** requires identified critical operations, set **impact tolerances**, and *tested* continuity plans; the EU's **DORA** is similar. The bar has moved from 'do you have a plan?' to 'have you *proven* you can keep critical services running through severe disruption?'

And the golden thread once more: resilience is ultimately a **culture**. When leadership funds the drills, takes the lessons seriously, and treats 'what if this breaks?' as a normal question, the organisation genuinely bends-not-breaks. When continuity is a binder produced for the auditor and never tested, it shatters at the first real shock.
bridge: So at work, you can cut through any continuity reassurance with the auditor's question: *when did we last actually test this, and what did we fix afterwards?* A plan never rehearsed is a hope, not a capability. True resilience is built by exercising plans, learning from the gaps, keeping them current, and a culture that treats 'what if?' as routine — not by the page count of an untested binder.

interactions.title: 'When did you last test it?'
interactions.prompt: A firm has a beautiful 200-page continuity plan it has never tested. How much is it worth?

options.label: A lot — it's thorough and professionally written.
options.feedback: Length and polish aren't capability. An untested plan is an assumption — it'll fail in ways you can only find by running it. The page count is false comfort.

options.label: Little — an untested plan is a hope, not a proven capability.
options.feedback: Exactly. Like a fire escape no one has walked, it looks fine until the real emergency exposes the gaps. Testing is what turns a binder into actual resilience.

options.label: It's fine as long as it's never needed.
options.feedback: You can't choose when it's needed — that's the whole point of continuity. And the first time you 'use' an untested plan is the worst possible time to discover its flaws.

interactions.title: Real resilience or paperwork?
interactions.prompt: Sort each practice by whether it builds genuine resilience or just produces paperwork.

items.label: A thick plan that's never been tested
A plan listing suppliers you dropped years ago
Board oversight and a named plan owner
Writing the plan once and filing it forever

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “A resilient organisation isn't the one with the thickest continuity binder — it's the one that actually rehearses its plans, fixes the gaps, keeps them current, and treats 'what if this breaks?' as a normal question, because an untested plan is just a hope.”

checkpoint.q: An untested business continuity plan is best described as…
options: An assumption/hope that may fail in ways you haven't discovered
checkpoint.explain: You only know a plan works by running it — like a fire escape no one has ever walked.

options: Talking through a crisis scenario to find gaps before a real event
checkpoint.explain: It rehearses 'what would we do if…?' surfacing missing steps, contacts, and owners in advance.

checkpoint.q: Operational resilience regimes (e.g. APRA CPS 230) require firms to…
options: Prove they can keep critical services running through severe disruption, with tested plans
checkpoint.explain: The bar moved from 'have a plan' to 'demonstrate tested resilience within set impact tolerances'.
